narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 60% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This model looks at key aspects of your background, like your academic rankings, test scores, and personal details, to forecast bar success. In your case, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well below the typical student's 5.5—had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction firmly toward failure since lower rankings signal struggles that often carry over to the bar. Next in impact was your LSAT score of 31, lower than the average 37, which similarly raised concerns about exam readiness. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also under the average 5.7, played a close third role in tipping the scales against you. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.1, far below the usual 3.2, followed as another key factor highlighting inconsistent performance. Even your upper-middle family income, while solid and around average, contributed slightly toward the failure prediction in this context. Your racial background as White, male gender, and full-time study plans—all quite common in the applicant pool—had the smallest influences but still nudged the model toward expecting challenges. Overall, these academic metrics painted a picture riskier than most applicants, whose stronger profiles predict passing with higher confidence. The good news is some of these are changeable: retaking the LSAT to aim above 37 could make a big difference, as could building better study habits shown through improved grades in any ongoing programs. Focusing on rigorous bar prep courses tailored to your weaknesses might also shift future predictions in your favor. Keep pushing—you have time to strengthen these areas and reapply with a more competitive story.
